primary.justification: The chapter's primary structural activity is explaining and analysing the major historical change from the mahājanapadas era to the Mauryan Empire — and its profound impact on Indian society politically (imperial governance, Aśhoka's transformation to dhamma), economically (trade, guilds, coins), culturally (spread of Buddhism, Brahmi script), and socially. Every named section — Rise of Magadha, Nanda Dynasty, Arrival of Greeks, Mighty Mauryas, Aśhoka, Life in the Mauryan Period, Fragile Nature of Empires — addresses a dimension of this change or its societal impact. Remove this competency and the chapter's fundamental organising purpose dissolves. The chapter is firmly in the History sub-discipline.

Two architecturally distinct named sections develop this competency substantively: (1) 'Trade, Trade Routes and Guilds' is a dedicated section analysing how guilds (śhrenīs) organised the nature of work — craftsmen, traders, and agriculturists forming self-governing bodies with shared resources, skills, and markets; (2) 'Life in the Mauryan Period' is a dedicated section describing changes in habitation (Pāṭaliputra as planned urban centre), the nature of work (agriculture, artisans, blacksmiths, potters, jewellers), and sociocultural achievements (art, architecture, edicts). Together these constitute substantive development of C-3.1 across multiple distinct named sections, satisfying Rule 5(b).
